questions.question: Define Mathematical Morphology and state its fundamental basis.
keyPoints: Mathematical Morphology is a theory for analysis and processing of geometrical structures
Based on set theory, lattice theory, topology, and random functions
Used for extracting image components for representation and description of region shapes
Provides tools for boundaries, skeletons, and convex hulls

questions.question: Explain the operation of erosion in binary morphological image processing with mathematical definition.
keyPoints: Erosion shrinks objects by removing boundary pixels
Mathematical definition: A ⊖ B = {x | (B)_x ⊆ A}
All points x where translated B is completely contained in A
Eliminates small protrusions and fills gaps
Separates touching objects

questions.question: Define dilation and explain its effect on binary images.
keyPoints: Dilation expands objects by adding boundary pixels
Mathematical definition: A ⊕ B = {x | (B)_x ∩ A ≠ ∅}
All points x where translated B overlaps with A
Fills small holes and connects nearby objects
Complementary to erosion

questions.question: What is the duality property between erosion and dilation? State the mathematical expressions.
keyPoints: Erosion and dilation are duals with respect to set complement
Enables solving problems by switching between operations
B̂ denotes reflection of structuring element B

questions.question: Define and explain the Reflection and Translation operations in morphological processing.
keyPoints: Reflection: B̂ = {(-x,-y) | (x,y) ∈ B} - rotate by 180° about origin
Translation: (A)_x = {a + x | a ∈ A} - shift all points by vector x
Reflection is used in duality formulas and hit-or-miss transform
Translation is used in defining erosion and dilation operations
Both are fundamental to morphological algebra

questions.question: Derive and explain the duality property between erosion and dilation. Show how this property can be used to simplify morphological operations.
keyPoints: Start with definitions of erosion and dilation
Use set theory: (A ⊖ B)^c = {x | (B)_x ⊈ A} = {x | (B)_x ∩ A^c ≠ ∅} = A^c ⊕ B̂
Similar derivation for (A ⊕ B)^c
Explain practical implications: solving foreground problems via background operations
Example: Finding boundary = dilation - erosion = A ⊕ B minus A ⊖ B
Use duality to convert erosion problems to dilation problems and vice versa

questions.question: Explain the concept of Structuring Element in detail. How does its size and shape affect morphological operations? Provide examples.
keyPoints: SE is a small binary image/template used to probe the image
Has defined origin (usually center)
Shapes: square, disk, cross, line - each detects different features
Size: larger SE = more aggressive shrinking/growing
3×3 square: removes/adds 1 pixel layer
5×5 square: removes/adds 2 pixel layers
Flat vs non-flat: flat for binary, non-flat for grayscale
Choice of SE depends on application and desired feature size

questions.question: Differentiate between binary and grayscale morphological operations.
keyPoints: Binary: operates on binary images using set theory operations
Grayscale: extends to intensity values, considers pixel values
Binary erosion/dilation: set containment/overlap test
Grayscale erosion: local minimum filter with SE
Grayscale dilation: local maximum filter with SE
Non-flat SEs used for grayscale have their own pixel values
Applications differ: binary for shape, grayscale for texture
